evidence_quotes.quote: Risk of misclassification of speech: Expressions of opinion or values consistent with the constitution of a Member State, as well as moral or religious convictions, could be wrongly classified as influencer marketing.

Overly broad rules on addictive design: The broad definitions related to manipulative, deceptive, or addictive design, as well as influencer communication, could unintentionally restrict the legitimate activities of organizations, educational, civic and other initiatives.
Risk of ideological bias and uneven enforcement: Terms such as vulnerability or harm may be defined in ways that stigmatize traditional beliefs or moral viewpoints.
Vague or ideologically charged definitions could lead to inconsistent application of the law and legal uncertainty for organizations and community initiatives operating online.

Protection of freedom of expression: A provision should be included stating that the DFA shall not restrict the lawful expression of opinions and beliefs protected under Articles 10 and 11 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.
Protection of parental rights: All measures relating to childrens digital use should respect the primary role of parents in their upbringing and education, in line with Article 14(3) of the Charter.

Fundamental rights impact assessment: Before adopting delegated acts or implementing guidelines, the European Commission should carry out a fundamental rights impact assessment, covering freedom of expression, conscience, and belief.

The Digital Fairness Act should promote ethical and transparent digital practices while ensuring full respect for the fundamental rights and freedoms of all citizens within the European digital space.

paragraphs.text: The Association Society and Values appreciates the efforts of the European Commission to strengthen consumer protection in the digital environment particularly for children and minors against manipulative and misleading online practices through the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA). At the same time, we consider it essential that the DFA include clear safeguards for the protection of freedom of expression, parental rights, and fundamental rights, and that a fundamental rights impact assessment be conducted at EU level prior to its adoption. Key Concerns Risk of misclassification of speech: Expressions of opinion or values consistent with the constitution of a Member State, as well as moral or religious convictions, could be wrongly classified as influencer marketing. Overly broad rules on addictive design: The broad definitions related to manipulative, deceptive, or addictive design, as well as influencer communication, could unintentionally restrict the legitimate activities of organizations, educational, civic and other initiatives. Risk of ideological bias and uneven enforcement: Terms such as vulnerability or harm may be defined in ways that stigmatize traditional beliefs or moral viewpoints. Vague or ideologically charged definitions could lead to inconsistent application of the law and legal uncertainty for organizations and community initiatives operating online. Proposed Amendments Protection of freedom of expression: A provision should be included stating that the DFA shall not restrict the lawful expression of opinions and beliefs protected under Articles 10 and 11 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union. Protection of parental rights: All measures relating to childrens digital use should respect the primary role of parents in their upbringing and education, in line with Article 14(3) of the Charter. Digital protection mechanisms should support families rather than replace or limit parental rights. Fundamental rights impact assessment: Before adopting delegated acts or implementing guidelines, the European Commission should carry out a fundamental rights impact assessment, covering freedom of expression, conscience, and belief. The Digital Fairness Act should promote ethical and transparent digital practices while ensuring full respect for the fundamental rights and freedoms of all citizens within the European digital space.
